one_line_takeaway: A large-scale C/C++ benchmark showing that simple sequence-based detectors outperform structurally-complex graph-based ones and far outperform ChatGPT at vulnerability detection, yet all remain low-F1, unstable to trivial code perturbations, and poorly separable — underscoring that current learning-based VD is far from deployment-ready.
key_finding: Sequence-based models beat graph-based models on every metric, and ChatGPT is not yet competent for VD. On the full MegaVul test set (Table 3), LineVul leads on Accuracy 0.962, Precision 0.117, and F1 0.195, while SVulD leads on Recall 0.637; the best graph model IVDetect reaches only F1 0.141, and ChatGPT (best-prompt ICL+Random_repo) gets F1 0.078 with Recall 0.125. Note all precision/F1 values are low because the dataset is highly imbalanced (~3.6% vulnerable), so LineVul's 0.962 accuracy is misleading. Detectors are also unstable to trivial semantic-preserving edits (IVDetect F1 drops 61.2%-82.5%; sequence models change <8%), and interpretability shows every model overlaps heavily between vulnerable/clean functions, focusing mainly on Function Call and Field Expression statements.
core_contribution: An extensive empirical study (not a new detector) that benchmarks five SOTA learning-based function-level vulnerability-detection models plus ChatGPT (GPT-3.5 Turbo) on a single large real-world C/C++ dataset (MegaVul), organized around seven research questions in five dimensions: capability, interpretation, stability, ease-of-use, and economy. Its own contribution is the head-to-head comparison and the resulting takeaways (Table 1), including the first systematic characterization of ChatGPT's VD ability, of model ease-of-use/deployment cost, and of vulnerability-type specialization. It does NOT propose MegaVul (that is prior work by the same group, Ni et al. [38]) nor any of the detectors it evaluates; those are cited baselines.
threat_model: The paper studies defensive tooling: automated detectors that flag vulnerable C/C++ functions before release. The security-relevant weaknesses it surfaces in its OWN experiments are (1) instability of detectors to semantics-preserving code perturbations (comment insertion/removal, dead-code insertion, identifier renaming) — an implicit adversarial-robustness / evasion concern, graph-based models being especially fragile; and (2) a privacy-leakage risk when using a closed-source cloud LLM (ChatGPT) since code must be sent off-premises, whereas the open-source detectors can run on the user's own server. The paper does not construct a formal attacker model; robustness and privacy are examined descriptively.
limitations: C/C++ only (no Java/Python), so generalization is unproven. ChatGPT results are from a single closed, non-reproducible GPT-3.5-Turbo version on a sampled subset, not the full test set. Devign had to be re-implemented (code unavailable), risking replication fidelity. The extreme class imbalance makes absolute F1/precision numbers hard to interpret and inflates accuracy. Only ChatGPT represents the 'LLM' category — no open-weight or code-specialized LLMs (e.g., CodeLlama) are tested despite the 'era of LLMs' framing. Interpretability conclusions rely on differing explainer tools across model families, complicating cross-model comparison.

datasets_used: MegaVul C/C++ (Oct-2023 release; 736 projects, 5,714 CVE IDs, 159 CWE IDs, 6,437 commits, 14,216 vulnerable / 377,185 non-vulnerable functions; 391,401 functions retained after Joern parsing for evaluation)
Statistically sampled MegaVul subset for ChatGPT: 1,039 functions (95% confidence, 3% interval), drawn from the 38,749-function test set
Four semantics-preserving variant test sets derived from MegaVul (remove comments, insert comments, insert irrelevant code, rename all identifiers)
CWE Top-25 Most Dangerous Software Weaknesses (2023 list) used for the danger-weighted analysis
quantitative_results: Best-prompt ChatGPT: CoT+Diversity gives highest Recall 0.375 (Acc 0.733); ICL+Random_repo gives best Precision 0.057 and F1 0.078; Zero-Shot has highest Accuracy 0.977 but predicts nearly everything clean (Recall 0)
Top-25 dangerous-CWE 'wins' (out of 628): SVulD 415, Devign 397, LineVul 394, IVDetect 380, ReVeal 302, ChatGPT 3/18
Stability F1 drop under rename-all-identifiers: IVDetect 82.5% down, Devign 38.4% down, ReVeal 22.0% down, LineVul 6.3% down, SVulD 7.9% up; graph-based unstable, sequence-based robust
Economy (dollar cost): ChatGPT $0.6385 (cheapest), SVulD $1.7792, Devign $2.8056, ReVeal $4.4378, IVDetect $4.8821, LineVul $6.1712
Model parameters: graph models ~0.97-1.09M; LineVul 124.65M; SVulD 125.93M; ChatGPT 175,000M (175B)
Preprocessing time: graph models 3,563-7,103s (Joern etc.); sequence models 0s; ChatGPT 1,263s
